item.title: Hey can I get a fuckin uuhhhhhhhh
item.content:encoded: This has a real Douglas Adams vibe to it; I was laughing all through the article. I think my favourite thing is actually how the lack of any backstory actively contributes, because wondering what led to a cognitohazard cheeseburger floating in the forest is in itself the anomaly.

item.title: Re: Mmm...I'll take my status quo with double bacon, hold the pickles.
item.content:encoded: it might be part of its effect to secretly make it so it cant be referred to as anything else

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it might transfer its effect to the person who consumes it, this would mean that observers of this person would want to change them to be as "normal" as possible, this could get really weird as the idea of a "normal" human is actually quite absurd.

(this would then only get worse after their death, as most people might become preoccupied with returning them to life)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i think this one is really cool. it seems to basically output a cognitohazard that make people want to make it "normal". i like any scp that tries to define "normal". "normal" is quite a complicated concept in this fiction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What would happen if you eat it?(if it was real)

Also +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm now wondering if this whole thing is a double-bluff, and it isn't a burger at all!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, it gives a lot of food for thought.

I can also see how you're kind of deconstructing the basic conceit of the Foundation and containment. Interesting stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

The final note really sealed the deal for me. And here's why:

+ Show
- Hide

I'm not sure if you meant it this way, but by adding:

Naturally, none of this goes in the official SCP file. The totally hypothetical one, of course.

You've got sort of a double meaning here. This could read as, "None of this goes in the SCP file, which as we all know is hypothetical anyway, wink wink." This could also read as, "None of this goes in the SCP file, but of course it goes in the fake hypothetical file."

Which, since we are reading this in an SCP file, would imply that the whole article we've just read is fake, and there is no cheeseburger, but there is something else with similar attributes requiring similar containment procedures.

As scarhaver mentioned above, it's kind of a mental riddle, and I guess I really like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I CogHaz cheezburger?

Best pun I've heard all day.

item.title: Mmm...I'll take my status quo with double bacon, hold the pickles.
item.content:encoded:
You know what they call that Quarter Pounder with cheese in SCP Foundation containment? Well, they go by the metric system, thy wouldn't know what the fuck a quarter-pounder is…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On 2015-04-10, Chinese artist Ai Weiwei was contracted as a containment consultant for SCP-3808.

Welp, +1.

(Extra +1 in my heart for Dr. Graff.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God, this is just a really good classic containment puzzle with an interesting and clever solution and it feels so… complete as that. I really like this burger. Thank you for writing this burger article and then showing me the burger article that you wrote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because sometimes things just are and there's no goddamn reason to tack on a lackluster backstory to every second anomaly in the hope that it'll make up for a lack of focus or thematic coherence, padding out a concept with addenda and recovery do nothing t

The use of artistic intent as containment reminds me in a good way of Gaffney's SCP-1474. Also, it's nice seeing Vanessa Graff pop up again. Fitting appearance for the character.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I CogHaz cheezburger?

+1 for a delicious variation on a Borgesian zahir.

item.title: borger
item.content:encoded:
